{{Infobox ancient site |name = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |native_name = 埼玉古墳群 |alternate_name = |image = Inariyama Kofun and Shogun-yama Kofun (Gyoda)-2.jpg |alt = |caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan |map_alt = location in Japan |map_size = 270px |map_caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |relief = 1 |coordinates = {{coord|35|07|42|N|139|28|36|E|region:JP-11|display=inline}} |location = [[Gyōda, Saitama]], [[Japan]] |region = [[Kantō region]] |type = kofun |part_of = |length = |width = |area = |height = |builder = |material = |built = 5th to 7th centuries AD |abandoned = |epochs = [[Kofun period]] |cultures = |dependency_of = |occupants = |event = |excavations = |archaeologists = |condition = |ownership = |management = |public_access = Yes (museum) |website = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Saikitama Kofun Cluster'''|埼玉古墳群|Sakitama kofun-gun}} is a group of [[kofun|Kofuns]] located in the city of [[Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the [[Asuka period]], when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.[[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]] According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in [[Musashi Province]] for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the [[Musashi Kokufu]] was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of [[Kokubunji, Tokyo]]. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the [[Edo period]] and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then [[Inariyama Kofun]] was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the [[Inariyama Sword]]). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''[[minka]]'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]], as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]], as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

{{Infobox ancient site |name = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |native_name = 埼玉古墳群 |alternate_name = |image = Inariyama Kofun and Shogun-yama Kofun (Gyoda)-2.jpg |alt = |caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan |map_alt = location in Japan |map_size = 270px |map_caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |relief = 1 |coordinates = {{coord|35|07|42|N|139|28|36|E|region:JP-11|display=inline}} |location = [[Gyōda, Saitama]], [[Japan]] |region = [[Kantō region]] |type = kofun |part_of = |length = |width = |area = |height = |builder = |material = |built = 5th to 7th centuries AD |abandoned = |epochs = [[Kofun period]] |cultures = |dependency_of = |occupants = |event = |excavations = |archaeologists = |condition = |ownership = |management = |public_access = Yes (museum) |website = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Saikitama Kofun Cluster'''|埼玉古墳群|Sakitama kofun-gun}} is a group of [[kofun|Kofuns]] located in the city of [[Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the [[Asuka period]], when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.[[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]] According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in [[Musashi Province]] for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the [[Musashi Kokufu]] was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of [[Kokubunji, Tokyo]]. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the [[Edo period]] and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then [[Inariyama Kofun]] was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the [[Inariyama Sword]]). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''[[minka]]'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]], as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the [[Asuka period]], when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.[[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]] According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in [[Musashi Province]] for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the [[Musashi Kokufu]] was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of [[Kokubunji, Tokyo]]. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the [[Edo period]] and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then [[Inariyama Kofun]] was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the [[Inariyama Sword]]). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''[[minka]]'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]], as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

{{Infobox ancient site |name = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |native_name = 埼玉古墳群 |alternate_name = |image = Inariyama Kofun and Shogun-yama Kofun (Gyoda)-2.jpg |alt = |caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan |map_alt = location in Japan |map_size = 270px |map_caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |relief = 1 |coordinates = {{coord|35|07|42|N|139|28|36|E|region:JP-11|display=inline}} |location = [[Gyōda, Saitama]], [[Japan]] |region = [[Kantō region]] |type = kofun |part_of = |length = |width = |area = |height = |builder = |material = |built = 5th to 7th centuries AD |abandoned = |epochs = [[Kofun period]] |cultures = |dependency_of = |occupants = |event = |excavations = |archaeologists = |condition = |ownership = |management = |public_access = Yes (museum) |website = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Saikitama Kofun Cluster'''|埼玉古墳群|Sakitama kofun-gun}} is a group of [[kofun|Kofuns]] located in the city of [[Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the [[Asuka period]], when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.[[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]] According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in [[Musashi Province]] for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the [[Musashi Kokufu]] was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of [[Kokubunji, Tokyo]]. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the [[Edo period]] and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then [[Inariyama Kofun]] was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the [[Inariyama Sword]]). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''[[minka]]'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]], as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

{{Infobox ancient site |name = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |native_name = 埼玉古墳群 |alternate_name = |image = Inariyama Kofun and Shogun-yama Kofun (Gyoda)-2.jpg |alt = |caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan |map_alt = location in Japan |map_size = 270px |map_caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |relief = 1 |coordinates = {{coord|35|07|42|N|139|28|36|E|region:JP-11|display=inline}} |location = [[Gyōda, Saitama]], [[Japan]] |region = [[Kantō region]] |type = kofun |part_of = |length = |width = |area = |height = |builder = |material = |built = 5th to 7th centuries AD |abandoned = |epochs = [[Kofun period]] |cultures = |dependency_of = |occupants = |event = |excavations = |archaeologists = |condition = |ownership = |management = |public_access = Yes (museum) |website = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Saikitama Kofun Cluster'''|埼玉古墳群|Sakitama kofun-gun}} is a group of [[kofun|Kofuns]] located in the city of [[Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the [[Asuka period]], when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.[[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:qq 4]] According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in [[Musashi Province]] for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the [[Musashi Kokufu]] was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of [[Kokubunji, Tokyo]]. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the [[Edo period]] and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then [[Inariyama Kofun]] was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the [[Inariyama Sword]]). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''[[minka]]'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]], as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]], as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

{{Infobox ancient site |name = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |native_name = 埼玉古墳群 |alternate_name = |image = Inariyama Kofun and Shogun-yama Kofun (Gyoda)-2.jpg |alt = |caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan |map_alt = location in Japan |map_size = 270px |map_caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |relief = 1 |coordinates = {{coord|35|07|42|N|139|28|36|E|region:JP-11|display=inline}} |location = [[Gyōda, Saitama]], [[Japan]] |region = [[Kantō region]] |type = kofun |part_of = |length = |width = |area = |height = |builder = |material = |built = 5th to 7th centuries AD |abandoned = |epochs = [[Kofun period]] |cultures = |dependency_of = |occupants = |event = |excavations = |archaeologists = |condition = |ownership = |management = |public_access = Yes (museum) |website = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Saikitama Kofun Cluster'''|埼玉古墳群|Sakitama kofun-gun}} is a group of [[kofun|Kofuns]] located in the city of [[Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the [[Asuka period]], when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.[[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]] According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in [[Musashi Province]] for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the [[Musashi Kokufu]] was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of [[Kokubunji, Tokyo]]. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the [[Edo period]] and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then [[Inariyama Kofun]] was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the [[Inariyama Sword]]). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''[[minka]]'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]], as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the [[Asuka period]], when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{Ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.[[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]] According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in [[Musashi Province]] for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the [[Musashi Kokufu]] was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of [[Kokubunji, Tokyo]]. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the [[Edo period]] and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then [[Inariyama Kofun]] was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the [[Inariyama Sword]]). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''[[minka]]'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]], as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |native_name = 埼玉古墳群 |alternate_name = |image = Inariyama Kofun and Shogun-yama Kofun (Gyoda)-2.jpg |alt = |caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan |map_alt = location in Japan |map_size = 270px |map_caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |relief = 1 |coordinates = {{coord|35|07|42|N|139|28|36|E|region:JP-11|display=inline}} |location = [[Gyōda, Saitama]], [[Japan]] |region = [[Kantō region]] |type = kofun |part_of = |length = |width = |area = |height = |builder = |material = |built = 5th to 7th centuries AD |abandoned = |epochs = [[Kofun period]] |cultures = |dependency_of = |occupants = |event = |excavations = |archaeologists = |condition = |ownership = |management = |public_access = Yes (museum) |website = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Saikitama Kofun Cluster'''|埼玉古墳群|Sakitama kofun-gun}} is a group of [[kofun|Kofuns]] located in the city of [[Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the [[Asuka period]], when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.[[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]] According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in [[Musashi Province]] for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the [[Musashi Kokufu]] was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of [[Kokubunji, Tokyo]]. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the [[Edo period]] and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then [[Inariyama Kofun]] was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the [[Inariyama Sword]]). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''[[minka]]'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]], as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |native_name = 埼玉古墳群 |alternate_name = |image = Inariyama Kofun and Shogun-yama Kofun (Gyoda)-2.jpg |alt = |caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan |map_alt = location in Japan |map_size = 270px |map_caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |relief = 1 |coordinates = {{coord|35|07|42|N|139|28|36|E|region:JP-11|display=inline}} |location = [[Gyōda, Saitama]], [[Japan]] |region = [[Kantō region]] |type = kofun |part_of = |length = |width = |area = |height = |builder = |material = |built = 5th to 7th centuries AD |abandoned = |epochs = [[Kofun period]] |cultures = |dependency_of = |occupants = |event = |excavations = |archaeologists = |condition = |ownership = |management = |public_access = Yes (museum) |website = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Saikitama Kofun Cluster'''|埼玉古墳群|Sakitama kofun-gun}} is a group of [[kofun|Kofuns]] located in the city of [[Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the [[Asuka period]], when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.[[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]] According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in [[Musashi Province]] for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the [[Musashi Kokufu]] was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of [[Kokubunji, Tokyo]]. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the [[Edo period]] and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then [[Inariyama Kofun]] was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the [[Inariyama Sword]]). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''[[minka]]'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]], as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |native_name = 埼玉古墳群 |alternate_name = |image = Inariyama Kofun and Shogun-yama Kofun (Gyoda)-2.jpg |alt = |caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan |map_alt = location in Japan |map_size = 270px |map_caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |relief = 1 |coordinates = {{coord|35|07|42|N|139|28|36|E|region:JP-11|display=inline}} |location = [[Gyōda, Saitama]], [[Japan]] |region = [[Kantō region]] |type = kofun |part_of = |length = |width = |area = |height = |builder = |material = |built = 5th to 7th centuries AD |abandoned = |epochs = [[Kofun period]] |cultures = |dependency_of = |occupants = |event = |excavations = |archaeologists = |condition = |ownership = |management = |public_access = Yes (museum) |website = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Saikitama Kofun Cluster'''|埼玉古墳群|Sakitama kofun-gun}} is a group of [[kofun|Kofuns]] located in the city of [[Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the [[Asuka period]], when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.[[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]] According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in [[Musashi Province]] for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the [[Musashi Kokufu]] was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of [[Kokubunji, Tokyo]]. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the [[Edo period]] and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then [[Inariyama Kofun]] was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the [[Inariyama Sword]]). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''[[minka]]'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]], as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.
==List of ''kofun''== {| class="wikitable sortable" ! name || Length [m] || width[m] || Height [m] || Coordinates |- | [[Maruhakayama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|丸墓山古墳}}) || 105 || data-sort-value="100" | ≈100 || 18,9 || {{coord|36|7|45|N|139|28|42|E|region:JP-11_dim:105_type:mountain|name=Maruhakayama-Kofun}} |- | [[Inariyama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷山古墳}}) || 120 || 74 || 11,7 || {{coord|36|7|45|N|139|28|50|E|region:JP-11_dim:120_type:mountain|name=Inariyama Kofun}} |- | [[Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|行田二子山古墳}}) || 138 || 90 || 13,0 || {{coord|36|7|34|N|139|28|44|E|region:JP-11_dim:138_type:mountain|name=Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun}} |- | [[Teppōyama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|鉄砲山古墳}}) || 109 || 69 || {{0}}9,0 || {{coord|36|7|25.5|N|139|28|40.5|E|region:JP-11_dim:109_type:mountain|name=Teppōyama Kofun}} |- | {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} ({{lang|ja|将軍山古墳}}) || {{0}}90 || 68 || data-sort-value="08,2" | ≥8,2 || {{coord|36|7|38|N|139|28|52|E|region:JP-11_dim:90_type:mountain|name=Shōgunyama Kofun}} |- | [[Nakanoyama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|中の山古墳}}) || {{0}}79 || 44 || {{0}}5,1 || {{coord|36|7|20|N|139|28|41.5|E|region:JP-11_dim:79_type:mountain|name=Nakanoyama Kofun}} |- | [[Kawarazuka Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|瓦塚古墳}}) || {{0}}73 || 47 || {{0}}5,1 || {{coord|36|7|29|N|139|28|36|E|region:JP-11_dim:73_type:mountain|name=Kawarazuka Kofun}} |- | [[Okunoyama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|奥の山古墳}}) || {{0}}66 || 40 || {{0}}5,6 || {{coord|36|7|22|N|139|28|37.5|E|region:JP-11_dim:66_type:mountain|name=Okunoyama Kofun}} |- | [[Atagoyama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|愛宕山古墳}}) || {{0}}53 || 30 || {{0}}3,4 || {{coord|36|7|34|N|139|28|36.5|E|region:JP-11_dim:53_type:mountain|name=Atagoyama Kofun}} |- |}

A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]], as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |native_name = 埼玉古墳群 |alternate_name = |image = Inariyama Kofun and Shogun-yama Kofun (Gyoda)-2.jpg |alt = |caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan |map_alt = location in Japan |map_size = 270px |map_caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |relief = 1 |coordinates = {{coord|35|07|42|N|139|28|36|E|region:JP-11|display=inline}} |location = [[Gyōda, Saitama]], [[Japan]] |region = [[Kantō region]] |type = kofun |part_of = |length = |width = |area = |height = |builder = |material = |built = 5th to 7th centuries AD |abandoned = |epochs = [[Kofun period]] |cultures = |dependency_of = |occupants = |event = |excavations = |archaeologists = |condition = |ownership = |management = |public_access = Yes (museum) |website = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Saikitama Kofun Cluster'''|埼玉古墳群|Sakitama kofun-gun}} is a group of [[kofun|Kofuns]] located in the city of [[Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the [[Asuka period]], when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.[[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]] According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in [[Musashi Province]] for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the [[Musashi Kokufu]] was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of [[Kokubunji, Tokyo]]. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the [[Edo period]] and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then [[Inariyama Kofun]] was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the [[Inariyama Sword]]). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''[[minka]]'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]], as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.
==List of ''kofun''== {| class="wikitable sortable" ! name || Length [m] || width[m] || Height [m] || Coordinates |- | [[Maruhakayama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|丸墓山古墳}}) || 105 || data-sort-value="100" | ≈100 || 18,9 || {{coord|36|7|45|N|139|28|42|E|region:JP-11_dim:105_type:mountain|name=Maruhakayama-Kofun}} |- | [[Inariyama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷山古墳}}) || 120 || 74 || 11,7 || {{coord|36|7|45|N|139|28|50|E|region:JP-11_dim:120_type:mountain|name=Inariyama Kofun}} |- | [[Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|行田二子山古墳}}) || 138 || 90 || 13,0 || {{coord|36|7|34|N|139|28|44|E|region:JP-11_dim:138_type:mountain|name=Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun}} |- | [[Teppōyama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|鉄砲山古墳}}) || 109 || 69 || {{0}}9,0 || {{coord|36|7|25.5|N|139|28|40.5|E|region:JP-11_dim:109_type:mountain|name=Teppōyama Kofun}} |- | {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} ({{lang|ja|将軍山古墳}}) || {{0}}90 || 68 || data-sort-value="08,2" | ≥8,2 || {{coord|36|7|38|N|139|28|52|E|region:JP-11_dim:90_type:mountain|name=Shōgunyama Kofun}} |- | [[Nakanoyama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|中の山古墳}}) || {{0}}79 || 44 || {{0}}5,1 || {{coord|36|7|20|N|139|28|41.5|E|region:JP-11_dim:79_type:mountain|name=Nakanoyama Kofun}} |- | [[Kawarazuka Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|瓦塚古墳}}) || {{0}}73 || 47 || {{0}}5,1 || {{coord|36|7|29|N|139|28|36|E|region:JP-11_dim:73_type:mountain|name=Kawarazuka Kofun}} |- | [[Okunoyama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|奥の山古墳}}) || {{0}}66 || 40 || {{0}}5,6 || {{coord|36|7|22|N|139|28|37.5|E|region:JP-11_dim:66_type:mountain|name=Okunoyama Kofun}} |- | [[Atagoyama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|愛宕山古墳}}) || {{0}}53 || 30 || {{0}}3,4 || {{coord|36|7|34|N|139|28|36.5|E|region:JP-11_dim:53_type:mountain|name=Atagoyama Kofun}} |- |}

The {{nihongo|'''Saikitama Kofun Cluster'''|埼玉古墳群|Sakitama kofun-gun}} is a group of [[kofun|Kofuns]] located in the city of [[Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the [[Asuka period]], when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.[[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]] According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in [[Musashi Province]] for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the [[Musashi Kokufu]] was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of [[Kokubunji, Tokyo]]. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the [[Edo period]] and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then [[Inariyama Kofun]] was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the [[Inariyama Sword]]). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''[[minka]]'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]], as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

The {{nihongo|'''Saikitama Kofun Cluster'''|埼玉古墳群|Sakitama kofun-gun}} is a group of [[kofun|Kofuns]] located in the city of [[Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the [[Asuka period]], when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.[[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]] According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in [[Musashi Province]] for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the [[Musashi Kokufu]] was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of [[Kokubunji, Tokyo]]. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the [[Edo period]] and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then [[Inariyama Kofun]] was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the [[Inariyama Sword]]). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''[[minka]]'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.

{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |native_name = 埼玉古墳群 |alternate_name = |image = Inariyama Kofun and Shogun-yama Kofun (Gyoda)-2.jpg |alt = |caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan |map_alt = location in Japan |map_size = 270px |map_caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |relief = 1 |coordinates = {{coord|35|07|42|N|139|28|36|E|region:JP-11|display=inline}} |location = [[Gyōda, Saitama]], [[Japan]] |region = [[Kantō region]] |type = kofun |part_of = |length = |width = |area = |height = |builder = |material = |built = 5th to 7th centuries AD |abandoned = |epochs = [[Kofun period]] |cultures = |dependency_of = |occupants = |event = |excavations = |archaeologists = |condition = |ownership = |management = |public_access = Yes (museum) |website = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Saikitama Kofun Cluster'''|埼玉古墳群|Sakitama kofun-gun}} is a group of [[kofun|Kofuns]] located in the city of [[Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[Kantō region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the [[Asuka period]], when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.[[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]][[Category:11 4]] According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in [[Musashi Province]] for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the [[Musashi Kokufu]] was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of [[Kokubunji, Tokyo]]. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the [[Edo period]] and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then [[Inariyama Kofun]] was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the [[Inariyama Sword]]). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''[[minka]]'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]], as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]], as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.
