File:Inariyama Kofun and Shogun-yama Kofun (Gyoda).JPG|alt = |caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan |map_alt = location in Japan |map_size = 270px |map_caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |relief = 1 |coordinates ={{coord|35|07|42|N|139|28|36|E|region:JP-11|display=inline}}; {{Coord|36.12833333|N|139.47944444|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|location =[[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda, Saitama]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]; {{ill|Gyoda|WD=Q860659}}|region = [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] |type = kofun | part_of =; {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Park|WD=Q17224997}}; {{ill|Atagoyama Kofun|WD=Q11493844}}; {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|WD=Q11371449}}; {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|WD=Q3198272}}; {{ill|Kawarazuka Kofun|WD=Q11574186}}; {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|WD=Q11368078}}; {{ill|Nakanoyama Kofun|WD=Q11362767}}; {{ill|Okunoyama Kofun|WD=Q11445573}}; {{ill|Sengenzuka Kofun|WD=Q11557453}}; {{ill|Shōgun'yama Kofun|WD=Q11458633}}; {{ill|Teppōyama Kofun|WD=Q11649597}}; {{ill|Tobaguchiyama Kofun|WD=Q11496268}}|length = |width = |area = |height = |builder = |material = |built = 5th to 7th centuries AD |abandoned = |epochs = [[Kofun period]] |cultures = |dependency_of = |occupants = |event = |excavations = |archaeologists = |condition = |ownership = |management = |public_access = Yes (museum) |website = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}} |Historic Site= {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}; {{ill|Special Historic Site|WD=Q26764449}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Saikitama Kofun Cluster'''|埼玉古墳群|Sakitama kofun-gun}} is a group of [[kofun|Kofuns]] located in the city of [[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.
According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[:en:Konosu, Saitama|Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the [[:en:Musashi Kokufu|Musashi Kokufu]] was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of [[:en:Kokubunji, Tokyo|Kokubunji, Tokyo]]. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgunyama Kofun|1=Shōgun'yama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458633}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then [[:en:Inariyama Kofun|Inariyama Kofun]] was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|Inariyama Sword]]). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''[[:en:Minka|Minka]]'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]], as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then [[:en:Inariyama Kofun|Inariyama Kofun]] was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|Inariyama Sword]]). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''[[:en:Minka|Minka]]'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]], as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

File:Inariyama Kofun and Shogun-yama Kofun (Gyoda).JPG|alt = |caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan |map_alt = location in Japan |map_size = 270px |map_caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |relief = 1 |coordinates ={{coord|35|07|42|N|139|28|36|E|region:JP-11|display=inline}}; {{Coord|36.12833333|N|139.47944444|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|location =[[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda, Saitama]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]; {{ill|Gyoda|WD=Q860659}}|region = [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] |type = kofun | part_of =; {{ill|Tobaguchiyama Kofun|WD=Q11496268}}|length = |width = |area = |height = |builder = |material = |built = 5th to 7th centuries AD |abandoned = |epochs = [[Kofun period]] |cultures = |dependency_of = |occupants = |event = |excavations = |archaeologists = |condition = |ownership = |management = |public_access = Yes (museum) |website = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}} |Historic Site= {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}; {{ill|Special Historic Site|WD=Q26764449}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Saikitama Kofun Cluster'''|埼玉古墳群|Sakitama kofun-gun}} is a group of [[kofun|Kofuns]] located in the city of [[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.
According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[:en:Konosu, Saitama|Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the [[:en:Musashi Kokufu|Musashi Kokufu]] was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of [[:en:Kokubunji, Tokyo|Kokubunji, Tokyo]]. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgunyama Kofun|1=Shōgun'yama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458633}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then [[:en:Inariyama Kofun|Inariyama Kofun]] was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|Inariyama Sword]]). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''[[:en:Minka|Minka]]'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]], as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgunyama Kofun|1=Shōgun'yama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458633}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then [[:en:Inariyama Kofun|Inariyama Kofun]] was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|Inariyama Sword]]). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''[[:en:Minka|Minka]]'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]], as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

File:Inariyama Kofun and Shogun-yama Kofun (Gyoda).JPG|alt = |caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan |map_alt = location in Japan |map_size = 270px |map_caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |relief = 1 |coordinates ={{coord|35|07|42|N|139|28|36|E|region:JP-11|display=inline}}; {{Coord|36.12833333|N|139.47944444|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|location =[[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda, Saitama]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]; {{ill|Gyoda|WD=Q860659}}|region = [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] |type = kofun | part_of =; {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Park|WD=Q17224997}}; {{ill|Atagoyama Kofun|WD=Q11493844}}; {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|WD=Q11371449}}; {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|WD=Q3198272}}; {{ill|Kawarazuka Kofun|WD=Q11574186}}; {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|WD=Q11368078}}; {{ill|Nakanoyama Kofun|WD=Q11362767}}; {{ill|Okunoyama Kofun|WD=Q11445573}}; {{ill|Sengenzuka Kofun|WD=Q11557453}}; {{ill|Shōgun'yama Kofun|WD=Q11458633}}; {{ill|Teppōyama Kofun|WD=Q11649597}}; {{ill|Tobaguchiyama Kofun|WD=Q11496268}}|length = |width = |area = |height = |builder = |material = |built = 5th to 7th centuries AD |abandoned = |epochs = [[Kofun period]] |cultures = |dependency_of = |occupants = |event = |excavations = |archaeologists = |condition = |ownership = |management = |public_access = Yes (museum) |website = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}} |Historic Site= {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}; {{ill|Special Historic Site|WD=Q26764449}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Saikitama Kofun Cluster'''|埼玉古墳群|Sakitama kofun-gun}} is a group of [[kofun|Kofuns]] located in the city of [[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.
According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[:en:Konosu, Saitama|Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the [[:en:Musashi Kokufu|Musashi Kokufu]] was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of [[:en:Kokubunji, Tokyo|Kokubunji, Tokyo]]. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgunyama Kofun|1=Shōgun'yama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458633}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then [[:en:Inariyama Kofun|Inariyama Kofun]] was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|Inariyama Sword]]). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''[[:en:Minka|Minka]]'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]], as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.
==List of ''kofun''== {| class="wikitable sortable" ! name || Length [m] || width[m] || Height [m] || Coordinates |- | [[Maruhakayama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|丸墓山古墳}}) || 105 || data-sort-value="100" | ≈100 || 18,9 || {{coord|36|7|45|N|139|28|42|E|region:JP-11_dim:105_type:mountain|name=Maruhakayama-Kofun}} |- | [[:en:Inariyama Kofun|Inariyama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|稲荷山古墳}}) || 120 || 74 || 11,7 || {{coord|36|7|45|N|139|28|50|E|region:JP-11_dim:120_type:mountain|name=Inariyama Kofun}} |- | [[Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|行田二子山古墳}}) || 138 || 90 || 13,0 || {{coord|36|7|34|N|139|28|44|E|region:JP-11_dim:138_type:mountain|name=Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun}} |- | [[Teppōyama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|鉄砲山古墳}}) || 109 || 69 || {{0}}9,0 || {{coord|36|7|25.5|N|139|28|40.5|E|region:JP-11_dim:109_type:mountain|name=Teppōyama Kofun}} |- | {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgunyama Kofun|1=Shōgun'yama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458633}} ({{lang|ja|将軍山古墳}}) || {{0}}90 || 68 || data-sort-value="08,2" | ≥8,2 || {{coord|36|7|38|N|139|28|52|E|region:JP-11_dim:90_type:mountain|name=Shōgunyama Kofun}} |- | [[Nakanoyama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|中の山古墳}}) || {{0}}79 || 44 || {{0}}5,1 || {{coord|36|7|20|N|139|28|41.5|E|region:JP-11_dim:79_type:mountain|name=Nakanoyama Kofun}} |- | [[Kawarazuka Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|瓦塚古墳}}) || {{0}}73 || 47 || {{0}}5,1 || {{coord|36|7|29|N|139|28|36|E|region:JP-11_dim:73_type:mountain|name=Kawarazuka Kofun}} |- | [[Okunoyama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|奥の山古墳}}) || {{0}}66 || 40 || {{0}}5,6 || {{coord|36|7|22|N|139|28|37.5|E|region:JP-11_dim:66_type:mountain|name=Okunoyama Kofun}} |- | [[Atagoyama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|愛宕山古墳}}) || {{0}}53 || 30 || {{0}}3,4 || {{coord|36|7|34|N|139|28|36.5|E|region:JP-11_dim:53_type:mountain|name=Atagoyama Kofun}} |- |}

However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.
According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[:en:Konosu, Saitama|Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the [[:en:Musashi Kokufu|Musashi Kokufu]] was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of [[:en:Kokubunji, Tokyo|Kokubunji, Tokyo]]. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgunyama Kofun|1=Shōgun'yama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458633}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then [[:en:Inariyama Kofun|Inariyama Kofun]] was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|Inariyama Sword]]). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''[[:en:Minka|Minka]]'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]], as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

The {{nihongo|'''Saikitama Kofun Cluster'''|埼玉古墳群|Sakitama kofun-gun}} is a group of [[kofun|Kofuns]] located in the city of [[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]], in the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.
According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[:en:Konosu, Saitama|Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the [[:en:Musashi Kokufu|Musashi Kokufu]] was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of [[:en:Kokubunji, Tokyo|Kokubunji, Tokyo]]. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgunyama Kofun|1=Shōgun'yama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458633}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then [[:en:Inariyama Kofun|Inariyama Kofun]] was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|Inariyama Sword]]). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''[[:en:Minka|Minka]]'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]], as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.
According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[:en:Konosu, Saitama|Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the [[:en:Musashi Kokufu|Musashi Kokufu]] was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of [[:en:Kokubunji, Tokyo|Kokubunji, Tokyo]]. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgunyama Kofun|1=Shōgun'yama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458633}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then [[:en:Inariyama Kofun|Inariyama Kofun]] was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the [[:en:Inariyama Sword|Inariyama Sword]]). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''[[:en:Minka|Minka]]'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]], as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

{{ill|Special Historic Site|WD=Q26764449}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Saikitama Kofun Cluster'''|埼玉古墳群|Sakitama kofun-gun}} is a group of [[kofun|Kofuns]] located in the city of [[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}}, when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.
According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province}} for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[:en:Konosu, Saitama|Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the {{ill|Musashi Kokufu|en|Musashi Kokufu|WD=Q11546339|lt=Musashi Kokufu}} was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of {{ill|Kokubunji, Tokyo|en|Kokubunji, Tokyo|WD=Q317834|lt=Kokubunji, Tokyo}}. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgunyama Kofun|1=Shōgun'yama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458633}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|en|Inariyama Kofun|WD=Q3198272|lt=Inariyama Kofun}} was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the {{ill|Inariyama Sword|en|Inariyama Sword|WD=Q856395|lt=Inariyama Sword}}). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''{{ill|Minka|en|Minka|WD=Q935445|lt=Minka}}'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO}} {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site}}, as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

{{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|WD=Q11368078}}; {{ill|Nakanoyama Kofun|WD=Q11362767}}; {{ill|Okunoyama Kofun|WD=Q11445573}}; {{ill|Sengenzuka Kofun|WD=Q11557453}}; {{ill|Shōgun'yama Kofun|WD=Q11458633}}; {{ill|Teppōyama Kofun|WD=Q11649597}}; {{ill|Tobaguchiyama Kofun|WD=Q11496268}}|length = |width = |area = |height = |builder = |material = |built = 5th to 7th centuries AD |abandoned = |epochs = [[Kofun period]] |cultures = |dependency_of = |occupants = |event = |excavations = |archaeologists = |condition = |ownership = |management = |public_access = Yes (museum) |website = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}} |Historic Site= {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}; {{ill|Special Historic Site|WD=Q26764449}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Saikitama Kofun Cluster'''|埼玉古墳群|Sakitama kofun-gun}} is a group of [[kofun|Kofuns]] located in the city of [[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}}, when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.
According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province}} for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[:en:Konosu, Saitama|Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the {{ill|Musashi Kokufu|en|Musashi Kokufu|WD=Q11546339|lt=Musashi Kokufu}} was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of {{ill|Kokubunji, Tokyo|en|Kokubunji, Tokyo|WD=Q317834|lt=Kokubunji, Tokyo}}. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgunyama Kofun|1=Shōgun'yama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458633}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|en|Inariyama Kofun|WD=Q3198272|lt=Inariyama Kofun}} was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the {{ill|Inariyama Sword|en|Inariyama Sword|WD=Q856395|lt=Inariyama Sword}}). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''{{ill|Minka|en|Minka|WD=Q935445|lt=Minka}}'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO}} {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site}}, as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

{{ill|Special Historic Site|WD=Q26764449}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Saikitama Kofun Cluster'''|埼玉古墳群|Sakitama kofun-gun}} is a group of [[kofun|Kofuns]] located in the city of [[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}}, when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.
According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province}} for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[:en:Konosu, Saitama|Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the {{ill|Musashi Kokufu|en|Musashi Kokufu|WD=Q11546339|lt=Musashi Kokufu}} was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of {{ill|Kokubunji, Tokyo|en|Kokubunji, Tokyo|WD=Q317834|lt=Kokubunji, Tokyo}}. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgunyama Kofun|1=Shōgun'yama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458633}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|en|Inariyama Kofun|WD=Q3198272|lt=Inariyama Kofun}} was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the {{ill|Inariyama Sword|en|Inariyama Sword|WD=Q856395|lt=Inariyama Sword}}). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''{{ill|Minka|en|Minka|WD=Q935445|lt=Minka}}'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO}} {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site}}, as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Sakitama-Kofungruppe]][[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[ja:埼玉古墳群]][[zh:埼玉古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Sakitama-Kofungruppe]][[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[ja:埼玉古墳群]][[zh:埼玉古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Sakitama-Kofungruppe]][[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[ja:埼玉古墳群]][[zh:埼玉古墳群]]
{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |native_name = 埼玉古墳群 |alternate_name = |image =Inariyama Kofun and Shogun-yama Kofun (Gyoda)-2.jpg; File:Inariyama Kofun and Shogun-yama Kofun (Gyoda).JPG|alt = |caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan |map_alt = location in Japan |map_size = 270px |map_caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |relief = 1 |coordinates ={{coord|35|07|42|N|139|28|36|E|region:JP-11|display=inline}}; {{Coord|36.12833333|N|139.47944444|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|location =[[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda, Saitama]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}; {{ill|Gyoda|WD=Q860659}}|region = {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} |type = kofun | part_of =; {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Park|WD=Q17224997}}; {{ill|Atagoyama Kofun|WD=Q11493844}}; {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|WD=Q11371449}}; {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|WD=Q3198272}}; {{ill|Kawarazuka Kofun|WD=Q11574186}}; {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|WD=Q11368078}}; {{ill|Nakanoyama Kofun|WD=Q11362767}}; {{ill|Okunoyama Kofun|WD=Q11445573}}; {{ill|Sengenzuka Kofun|WD=Q11557453}}; {{ill|Shōgun'yama Kofun|WD=Q11458633}}; {{ill|Teppōyama Kofun|WD=Q11649597}}; {{ill|Tobaguchiyama Kofun|WD=Q11496268}}|length = |width = |area = |height = |builder = |material = |built = 5th to 7th centuries AD |abandoned = |epochs = [[Kofun period]] |cultures = |dependency_of = |occupants = |event = |excavations = |archaeologists = |condition = |ownership = |management = |public_access = Yes (museum) |website = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}} |Historic Site= {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}; {{ill|Special Historic Site|WD=Q26764449}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Saikitama Kofun Cluster'''|埼玉古墳群|Sakitama kofun-gun}} is a group of [[kofun|Kofuns]] located in the city of [[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}, in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}}, when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.
According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province}} for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[:en:Konosu, Saitama|Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the {{ill|Musashi Kokufu|en|Musashi Kokufu|WD=Q11546339|lt=Musashi Kokufu}} was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of {{ill|Kokubunji, Tokyo|en|Kokubunji, Tokyo|WD=Q317834|lt=Kokubunji, Tokyo}}. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgunyama Kofun|1=Shōgun'yama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458633}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|en|Inariyama Kofun|WD=Q3198272|lt=Inariyama Kofun}} was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the {{ill|Inariyama Sword|en|Inariyama Sword|WD=Q856395|lt=Inariyama Sword}}). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''{{ill|Minka|en|Minka|WD=Q935445|lt=Minka}}'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO}} {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site}}, as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.
==List of ''kofun''== {| class="wikitable sortable" ! name || Length [m] || width[m] || Height [m] || Coordinates |- | [[Maruhakayama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|丸墓山古墳}}) || 105 || data-sort-value="100" | ≈100 || 18,9 || {{coord|36|7|45|N|139|28|42|E|region:JP-11_dim:105_type:mountain|name=Maruhakayama-Kofun}} |- | {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|en|Inariyama Kofun|WD=Q3198272|lt=Inariyama Kofun}} ({{lang|ja|稲荷山古墳}}) || 120 || 74 || 11,7 || {{coord|36|7|45|N|139|28|50|E|region:JP-11_dim:120_type:mountain|name=Inariyama Kofun}} |- | [[Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|行田二子山古墳}}) || 138 || 90 || 13,0 || {{coord|36|7|34|N|139|28|44|E|region:JP-11_dim:138_type:mountain|name=Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun}} |- | [[Teppōyama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|鉄砲山古墳}}) || 109 || 69 || {{0}}9,0 || {{coord|36|7|25.5|N|139|28|40.5|E|region:JP-11_dim:109_type:mountain|name=Teppōyama Kofun}} |- | {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgunyama Kofun|1=Shōgun'yama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458633}} ({{lang|ja|将軍山古墳}}) || {{0}}90 || 68 || data-sort-value="08,2" | ≥8,2 || {{coord|36|7|38|N|139|28|52|E|region:JP-11_dim:90_type:mountain|name=Shōgunyama Kofun}} |- | [[Nakanoyama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|中の山古墳}}) || {{0}}79 || 44 || {{0}}5,1 || {{coord|36|7|20|N|139|28|41.5|E|region:JP-11_dim:79_type:mountain|name=Nakanoyama Kofun}} |- | [[Kawarazuka Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|瓦塚古墳}}) || {{0}}73 || 47 || {{0}}5,1 || {{coord|36|7|29|N|139|28|36|E|region:JP-11_dim:73_type:mountain|name=Kawarazuka Kofun}} |- | [[Okunoyama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|奥の山古墳}}) || {{0}}66 || 40 || {{0}}5,6 || {{coord|36|7|22|N|139|28|37.5|E|region:JP-11_dim:66_type:mountain|name=Okunoyama Kofun}} |- | [[Atagoyama Kofun]] ({{lang|ja|愛宕山古墳}}) || {{0}}53 || 30 || {{0}}3,4 || {{coord|36|7|34|N|139|28|36.5|E|region:JP-11_dim:53_type:mountain|name=Atagoyama Kofun}} |- |}

However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.
According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province}} for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[:en:Konosu, Saitama|Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the {{ill|Musashi Kokufu|en|Musashi Kokufu|WD=Q11546339|lt=Musashi Kokufu}} was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of {{ill|Kokubunji, Tokyo|en|Kokubunji, Tokyo|WD=Q317834|lt=Kokubunji, Tokyo}}. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgunyama Kofun|1=Shōgun'yama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458633}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|en|Inariyama Kofun|WD=Q3198272|lt=Inariyama Kofun}} was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the {{ill|Inariyama Sword|en|Inariyama Sword|WD=Q856395|lt=Inariyama Sword}}). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''{{ill|Minka|en|Minka|WD=Q935445|lt=Minka}}'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO}} {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site}}, as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |native_name = 埼玉古墳群 |alternate_name = |image =Inariyama Kofun and Shogun-yama Kofun (Gyoda)-2.jpg; File:Inariyama Kofun and Shogun-yama Kofun (Gyoda).JPG|alt = |caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan |map_alt = location in Japan |map_size = 270px |map_caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |relief = 1 |coordinates ={{coord|35|07|42|N|139|28|36|E|region:JP-11|display=inline}}; {{Coord|36.12833333|N|139.47944444|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|location =[[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda, Saitama]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}; {{ill|Gyoda|WD=Q860659|1=Gyōda}}|region = {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} |type = kofun | part_of =; {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Park|WD=Q17224997|1=Sakitama Fudoki Hill}}; {{ill|Atagoyama Kofun|WD=Q11493844|1=Atagoyama Kofun}}; {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|WD=Q11371449|1=Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun}}; {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|WD=Q3198272|1=Inariyama Kofun}}; {{ill|Kawarazuka Kofun|WD=Q11574186|1=Kawarazuka Kofun}}; {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|WD=Q11368078|1=Maruhakayama Kofun}}; {{ill|Nakanoyama Kofun|WD=Q11362767|1=Nakanoyama Kofun}}; {{ill|Okunoyama Kofun|WD=Q11445573|1=Okunoyama Kofun}}; {{ill|Sengenzuka Kofun|WD=Q11557453|1=Sengenzuka Kofun}}; {{ill|Shōgun'yama Kofun|WD=Q11458633|1=Shōgun'yama Kofun}}; {{ill|Teppōyama Kofun|WD=Q11649597|1=Teppōyama Kofun}}; {{ill|Tobaguchiyama Kofun|WD=Q11496268|1=Tobaguchiyama Kofun}}|length = |width = |area = |height = |builder = |material = |built = 5th to 7th centuries AD |abandoned = |epochs = [[Kofun period]] |cultures = |dependency_of = |occupants = |event = |excavations = |archaeologists = |condition = |ownership = |management = |public_access = Yes (museum) |website = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}} |Historic Site= {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580|1=Historic Site of Japan}}; {{ill|Special Historic Site|WD=Q26764449|1=Special Historic Site}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Saikitama Kofun Cluster'''|埼玉古墳群|Sakitama kofun-gun}} is a group of [[kofun|Kofuns]] located in the city of [[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture|1=Saitama Prefecture}}, in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}}, when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.
According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}} for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[:en:Konosu, Saitama|Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the {{ill|Musashi Kokufu|en|Musashi Kokufu|WD=Q11546339|lt=Musashi Kokufu|1=Musashi Kokufu}} was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of {{ill|Kokubunji, Tokyo|en|Kokubunji, Tokyo|WD=Q317834|lt=Kokubunji, Tokyo|1=Kokubunji, Tokyo}}. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgunyama Kofun|1=Shōgun'yama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458633}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|en|Inariyama Kofun|WD=Q3198272|lt=Inariyama Kofun|1=Inariyama Kofun}} was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the {{ill|Inariyama Sword|en|Inariyama Sword|WD=Q856395|lt=Inariyama Sword|1=Inariyama Sword}}). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''{{ill|Minka|en|Minka|WD=Q935445|lt=Minka|1=Minka}}'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}, as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

{{ill|Sengenzuka Kofun|WD=Q11557453|1=Sengenzuka Kofun}}; {{ill|Shōgun'yama Kofun|WD=Q11458633|1=Shōgun'yama Kofun}}; {{ill|Teppōyama Kofun|WD=Q11649597|1=Teppōyama Kofun}}; {{ill|Tobaguchiyama Kofun|WD=Q11496268|1=Tobaguchiyama Kofun}}|length = |width = |area = |height = |builder = |material = |built = 5th to 7th centuries AD |abandoned = |epochs = [[Kofun period]] |cultures = |dependency_of = |occupants = |event = |excavations = |archaeologists = |condition = |ownership = |management = |public_access = Yes (museum) |website = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}} |Historic Site= {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580|1=Historic Site of Japan}}; {{ill|Special Historic Site|WD=Q26764449|1=Special Historic Site}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Saikitama Kofun Cluster'''|埼玉古墳群|Sakitama kofun-gun}} is a group of [[kofun|Kofuns]] located in the city of [[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture|1=Saitama Prefecture}}, in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}}, when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.
According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}} for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[:en:Konosu, Saitama|Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the {{ill|Musashi Kokufu|en|Musashi Kokufu|WD=Q11546339|lt=Musashi Kokufu|1=Musashi Kokufu}} was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of {{ill|Kokubunji, Tokyo|en|Kokubunji, Tokyo|WD=Q317834|lt=Kokubunji, Tokyo|1=Kokubunji, Tokyo}}. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgunyama Kofun|1=Shōgun'yama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458633}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|en|Inariyama Kofun|WD=Q3198272|lt=Inariyama Kofun|1=Inariyama Kofun}} was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the {{ill|Inariyama Sword|en|Inariyama Sword|WD=Q856395|lt=Inariyama Sword|1=Inariyama Sword}}). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''{{ill|Minka|en|Minka|WD=Q935445|lt=Minka|1=Minka}}'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}, as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.

Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}, as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.
