<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Sakitama-Kofungruppe]][[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[ja:埼玉古墳群]][[zh:埼玉古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Sakitama-Kofungruppe]][[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[ja:埼玉古墳群]][[zh:埼玉古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Sakitama-Kofungruppe]][[en:Sakitama Kofun Cluster]][[ja:埼玉古墳群]][[zh:埼玉古墳群]]
{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |native_name = 埼玉古墳群 |alternate_name = |image =Inariyama Kofun and Shogun-yama Kofun (Gyoda)-2.jpg; File:Inariyama Kofun and Shogun-yama Kofun (Gyoda).JPG|alt = |caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan |map_alt = location in Japan |map_size = 270px |map_caption = Saikitama Kofun Cluster |relief = 1 |coordinates ={{coord|35|07|42|N|139|28|36|E|region:JP-11|display=inline}}; {{Coord|36.12833333|N|139.47944444|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|location =[[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda, Saitama]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}; {{ill|Gyoda|WD=Q860659|1=Gyōda}}|region = {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} |type = kofun | part_of =; {{ill|Sakitama Kofun Park|WD=Q17224997|1=Sakitama Fudoki Hill}}; {{ill|Atagoyama Kofun|WD=Q11493844|1=Atagoyama Kofun}}; {{ill|Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun|WD=Q11371449|1=Gyōda Futagoyama Kofun}}; {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|WD=Q3198272|1=Inariyama Kofun}}; {{ill|Kawarazuka Kofun|WD=Q11574186|1=Kawarazuka Kofun}}; {{ill|Maruhakayama Kofun|WD=Q11368078|1=Maruhakayama Kofun}}; {{ill|Nakanoyama Kofun|WD=Q11362767|1=Nakanoyama Kofun}}; {{ill|Okunoyama Kofun|WD=Q11445573|1=Okunoyama Kofun}}; {{ill|Sengenzuka Kofun|WD=Q11557453|1=Sengenzuka Kofun}}; {{ill|Shōgun'yama Kofun|WD=Q11458633|1=Shōgun'yama Kofun}}; {{ill|Teppōyama Kofun|WD=Q11649597|1=Teppōyama Kofun}}; {{ill|Tobaguchiyama Kofun|WD=Q11496268|1=Tobaguchiyama Kofun}}|length = |width = |area = |height = |builder = |material = |built = 5th to 7th centuries AD |abandoned = |epochs = [[Kofun period]] |cultures = |dependency_of = |occupants = |event = |excavations = |archaeologists = |condition = |ownership = |management = |public_access = Yes (museum) |website = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}} |Historic Site= {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580|1=Historic Site of Japan}}; {{ill|Special Historic Site|WD=Q26764449|1=Special Historic Site}} }}
The {{nihongo|'''Saikitama Kofun Cluster'''|埼玉古墳群|Sakitama kofun-gun}} is a group of [[kofun|Kofuns]] located in the city of [[:en:Gyōda, Saitama|Gyōda]], {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture|1=Saitama Prefecture}}, in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The site was designated a National Historic Site in 1938, and was upgraded in status to a [[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/603 |title=埼玉古墳群|trans-title=Saikitama Kofun Cluster|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2020}}</ref> The site consists of nine large ''kofun'', which were built in the 5th to 7th centuries AD, i.e. from the late [[Kofun period]] into the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}}, when the construction of Kofuns was already out of fashion in western Japan.
==Overview== The area of "Sakitama" is the area which gave its name to become modern Saitama Prefecture. The site now consists of eight large {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and one large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun''. However, the area formerly included an additional 35 smaller {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular-type ''kofun''|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and one more {{ill|Square Kofun|lt=square-type|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{nihongo|''hōfun''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}), all of which have now been destroyed by urban encroachment and land development.
According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicle, in 534 AD, [[Emperor Ankan]] appointed Nao Kasahara as ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' after he won a succession battle in {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}} for that post, and miraculously a keyhole-shaped tomb appeared in Kasahara, Saitama Country (present-day [[:en:Konosu, Saitama|Konosu, Saitama]]) overnight, indicating the favor of the Yamato kingdom. This has led to a theory that the {{ill|Musashi Kokufu|en|Musashi Kokufu|WD=Q11546339|lt=Musashi Kokufu|1=Musashi Kokufu}} was originally in this location, although the ruins of a [[kokufu|provincial capital]] have been found in southern Musashi in the city of {{ill|Kokubunji, Tokyo|en|Kokubunji, Tokyo|WD=Q317834|lt=Kokubunji, Tokyo|1=Kokubunji, Tokyo}}. In any event, the tumuli in this area date from the end of the 5th century to the 7th century AD. The existence of these tumuli were known in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} and were commented upon in local histories, such as the "Shinpen Musashi Fudoki".
In 1893, the {{ill|Shōgunyama Kofun|lt=Shōgunyama Kofun|ja|将軍山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgunyama Kofun|1=Shōgun'yama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458633}} was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and in 1935 a survey of the area indicated that 11 keyhole-shaped and 11 circular-type mounds existed. In 1938, nine largest Kofuns were designated as national historic sites. Maintenance on these tumuli began in 1966, and in 1968, then {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|en|Inariyama Kofun|WD=Q3198272|lt=Inariyama Kofun|1=Inariyama Kofun}} was excavated, revealing a gold-inlaid iron sword (the {{ill|Inariyama Sword|en|Inariyama Sword|WD=Q856395|lt=Inariyama Sword|1=Inariyama Sword}}). The area was developed into the {{nihongo|'''Sakitama Kofun Park'''|さきたま風土記の丘|Sakitama Fudoki no Oka}}, and in addition to the tumuli, a number of traditional ''{{ill|Minka|en|Minka|WD=Q935445|lt=Minka|1=Minka}}'' farm houses were relocated to the historic park for preservation. A museum, the {{nihongo|'''[[Saitama Prefectural Museum of the Sakitama Ancient Kofuns]]'''|埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館|Saitama Kenritsu Sakitama Shiseki no Hakubutsukan}} was also opened.
Saitama Prefecture and the city of Gyōda are promoting the site for inclusion as a {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}, as a preliminary step, the site's designation was changed from a National Heritage Site to a Special National Heritage Site in 2020.
